message.content:
Decompose this research question into complementary search angles.

## Question
How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for.

## Task
Generate 5 distinct web search queries that together cover the question from different angles. Pick angles that suit the question's domain. Examples:
- broad/primary  · academic/technical  · recent news  · contrarian/skeptical  · practitioner/implementation
- For medical: anatomy · common causes · serious differentials · authoritative refs · red flags
- For tech: state-of-art · benchmarks · limitations · industry adoption · cost/tradeoffs

Make queries specific enough to surface high-signal results. Avoid redundancy.
Return: the question (verbatim or lightly normalized), a 1-2 sentence decomposition strategy, and the angles.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze this research question about how age affects comprehension of accelerated/time-compressed speech, then decompose it into complementary search angles.

input.question: How does age affect the ability to comprehend accelerated/time-compressed/fast speech ("speed listening") across the full human lifespan? Cover: (1) the developmental maturation curve in childhood/adolescence and how children compare to young adults; (2) the aging decline in older adults (50s-80s), its steepness and onset age; (3) mechanisms disentangling peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory/phonological-loop capacity, including whether the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility; (4) the peak age and plateau width for accelerated-speech comprehension; and (5) trainability/plasticity across age, including whether older adults can improve and how age of onset of blindness (early- vs late-blind) modulates the ultra-fast-speech advantage and visual-cortex plasticity. Provide quantitative thresholds (syllables/sec, words/min, % compression) and effect sizes by age band, prefer peer-reviewed sources, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is/isn't controlled.

angles.label: Aging decline + audibility-controlled mechanisms (primary/academic)
angles.query: time-compressed speech comprehension older adults age decline central auditory temporal processing controlling for hearing loss audibility presbycusis
angles.rationale: Targets the core aging question (angle 2) and the mechanism dissociation (angle 3) simultaneously — peer-reviewed studies that statistically control for pure-tone audibility are the highest-signal sources for separating peripheral from central/cognitive effects and quantifying onset/steepness of decline.

angles.label: Developmental maturation curve in children/adolescents
angles.query: developmental maturation time-compressed accelerated speech perception comprehension children adolescents versus young adults rate of speech processing age
angles.rationale: Covers angle 1 — surfaces child/adolescent psycholinguistic and audiology studies establishing at what age rapid-speech processing matures and how children's compression thresholds compare to young adults.

angles.label: Quantitative thresholds: syllables/sec, WPM, % compression by age band
angles.query: maximum intelligibility time-compressed speech rate syllables per second words per minute compression ratio threshold by age group quantitative
angles.rationale: Directly serves the quantitative-figures requirement and angles 2/4 — finds papers reporting numeric intelligibility limits and peak/plateau rates that can be benchmarked against the 13 syllables/second context figure.

angles.label: Cognitive processing speed & working-memory/phonological-loop mediators
angles.query: cognitive processing speed working memory phonological loop capacity mediates age differences rapid speech understanding effect size
angles.rationale: Isolates the cognitive arm of angle 3 — which mechanism dominates the age effect — by targeting individual-differences and mediation/regression studies reporting effect sizes for processing speed vs working memory.

angles.label: Training/plasticity in older adults & blindness onset (early- vs late-blind ultrafast speech)
angles.query: training plasticity older adults improve time-compressed speech comprehension early-blind late-blind ultra-fast speech visual cortex onset of blindness
angles.rationale: Covers angle 5 — combines auditory training/plasticity-in-aging literature with the blind ultra-fast-speech neuroimaging work (V1 recruitment, early- vs late-blind differences) that the question specifically calls out as a plasticity/age-of-onset modulator.

input.summary: Five non-overlapping angles map onto the question's five sub-parts: an audibility-controlled aging/mechanism query (the analytical crux), a developmental-curve query, a quantitative-thresholds query for syllables/sec and WPM benchmarking, a cognitive-mediators query for effect sizes, and a training-plus-blindness-plasticity query. Together they span childhood through old age, separate peripheral from central/cognitive causes, and capture both the peak/plateau and trainability dimensions while prioritizing peer-reviewed quantitative evidence.

content.text: I've decomposed the research question into five complementary search angles that together span the full lifespan and all five sub-questions, with minimal redundancy and a bias toward peer-reviewed, quantitative sources.
